details.text: وَالْقُرْآنِ ذِي الذِّكْرِ – here allah, swears by the qur’an that contains or possesses the reminder, but there is no answer (جَواب) for the qasm (oath) that was sworn i.e., i swear by the qur’an that … the answer has been omitted and left for the people to reach and deduce by themselves. the answer could be: “that allah alone deserves to be worshipped without partner”, or “indeed, he ﷺ is a messenger sent to you” or “the matter is not as the disbelievers say” etc. the following verse has been used to provide clues to its meaning.
Derivative: الْأَسْبَابِ refers to the means to attain something.

they (i.e., quraish) are just another force (جُندٌ مَّا) which is to be vanquished and defeated (مَهْزُومٌ) like the forces and nations (الْأَحْزَابِ) before them (throughout history) were vanquished.

Derivative: الْأَوْتَادِ means pegs or stakes which are driven in the ground to hold up a tent or structure. Here, it can literally refer to the pegs or stakes symbolising the armies that Fir’awn always had mobilised, or it can be symbolic of strength and being firmly consolidated by armies and force. It has also been said to refer to the stakes that Fir’awn used as a means to kill and torture.
Derivative: فَوَاقٍ – from فَاقَ and means إِفاقَة (recovery/awakening) or رُجُوع (return/repeat) or تَمَهُّل delay/postponement.
Derivative: قِطٌّ refers to a ledger. Here it is referring to the disbelievers’ accounts as they mock the Prophet ﷺ and say why don’t you just hurry and bring us our accounts now for us to see.

Derivative: أَوَّابٌ means to come back (to Allah) over and over again. It can also mean to sing the praises of Allah with words which contain choruses or with the meaning of echoing the Tasbeeh (Glorification) of Allah.
- ذَا الْأَيْدِ literally means the possessor of many hands. Here الْأَيْدِ is a metaphor for strength and power.
Derivative: الْإِشْرَاقِ refers to the early morning after sunset when the sun becomes bright.
Derivative: الْعَشِيِّ refers to the time between the Zawaal (noon) and Maghrib. Or the time approaching Maghrib towards the end of the day.
Derivative: مَحْشُورَةً – To be gathered together. Here referring to birds gathered in flocks.
Derivative: أَوَّابٌ – Echoing or repeating the glorification of Allah (Tasbeeh).

Derivative: شِقَاقٍ comes from the word شَقٌّ which means the existence of a separator between two matters or things. If something is in a شَقٌّ and another thing is in a شَقٌّ, they will not ever meet or come together. It is a side that has been separated from another. شِقَاقٍ means that you are in a schism and that you are never going to come together or meet with something. Here it refers to being in state of opposition and hostility which they are entrenched in so that they don’t reach the correct conclusion concerning the Oneness of Allah and His worship or the truthfulness of Muhammad ﷺ being a Messenger sent to them by Allah.
Derivative: عِزَّةٍ – Here عِزَّةٍ refers to pride and holding a sense of self-glory.
- فَصْلَ الْخِطَابِ is the address or speech that is clear and decisive. It also refers to the speech that passes judgement and verdicts in a decisive manner i.e., to have the final say.

Derivative: تَسَوَّرُوا from تَسَوَّرَ which means to scale a wall. It is used to express that the approach was not straight forward or through the main entrance, and that the place they were going to was not easily accessible.
Derivative: الْمِحْرَابَ refers to private quarters designated for worship.
Derivative: تُشْطِطْ from أَشَطَّ which can mean to defer and put off and also means to exceed the limits and be unjust. Hence, وَلا تُشْطِطْ means “And don’t be unjust”.
Derivative: فَزِعَ means to be frightened, alarmed or startled. It is a kind of fear that take you by surprise, where you can’t think clearly and startles you.
- Literally means: “The most even path” and here it means the correct path and outcome.
- “He overpowered me in the speech” with the meaning that He used His authority.

Derivative: الْخُلَطَاءِ means those who are mixed, intermingled and interact a lot with each other. It can refer to associates, partners in business, colleagues at work and people who live with each other or share in a matter among themselves.
- “To fall down Raaki’an” can mean to fall in prostration or to fall down onto one’s knees. رَكَعَ means to bend downward and as such can cover both bowing and prostration.
Derivative: مَآبٍ comes from آَبَ – يَؤُوبُ and means a place that you return to.
Derivative: زُلْفَىٰ means high standing and status, and it also means closeness.
Derivative: نَسُوا from نَسِيَ which means to forget but also to overlook and fail to acknowledge something and be neglectful of it.

Derivative: خَلِيفَةً – Refers to someone who succeeds a person before him, someone who takes care of or has been place in charge of great responsibility, someone who is in a chain of succession i.e., who succeeds someone and is succeeded himself. It is also the well-known title given to the leader of the Islamic Ummah. Here it refers to Allah causing Dawud (as) to succeed to the position of leadership, authority and responsibility.
Derivative: بَاطِلًا from بَطَلَ which means to be null and void, futile, worthless and false or invalid. The meaning here is without purpose.

Derivative: الْفُجَّارِ is from فَجَرَ which in origin carries the meaning of exploding and bursting out. فَاجِر and its plural فُجَّار was extracted from this meaning and it refers to the one who acts in a manner without any moral restraints whatsoever.

Derivative: مُبَارَكٌ from بَارَكَ – بَرَكَة. There are two aspects related to بَرَكَة (Barakah). The first is the increase in goodness or something that brings out more goodness than expected. The second meaning is to extract the good from something or someone that has the potential in it. For example, ماءٌ مُبارَك which does not only mean blessed water but rather water that can bring out the potential good from something like the seed. Similarly, كِتاب مُبارَك can imply a Book that has the potential of bringing out the good (i.e., in a person), just as it means the Book in itself is blessed.
Derivative: يَدَّبَّرُوا from تَدَبَّرَ which means to ponder and think deeply or to immerse yourself in the Book of Allah.

Derivative: لَاتَ – One view is that لَاتَ means the same as لَيْسَ or لَا carrying the meaning of negation or “not”. In this verse it would then mean: “And it’s not the time to make an escape” or “There is no time to escape”. There is a view that the addition of a تَاء to لا so that it becomes لَاتَ means a negation that is specific to that time instead of being general.
Derivative: الْجِيَادُ are very fast horses.

Derivative: الصَّافِنَاتُ – Is the plural of صَافِنَة which refers to a horse that stands on three legs and bends one leg at an angle as a show of its being well bred and trained and sign for its readiness. As such, الصَّافِنَاتُ refer to elite bred horses. Usually used for show and pomp.
Derivative: الْعَشِيِّ is the time between noon and sunset, or late afternoon (‘Asr) preceding sunset.

- Until it تَوَارَتْ (disappeared from sight) بِالْحِجَابِ (by its veil or cover). It refers to the sun and the veil refers to the night i.e., until when the sun disappeared from sight with the coming of the night. Another interpretation is that it refers to the horses disappearing from sight.
Derivative: جَسَدًا – Refers to a body that has no movement left in it as if it is paralysed or dead.
Derivative: فَتَنَّا – To put through a hard test or trial like اِبْتلاء.
Derivative: رُخَاءً gently and softly. Here, it can mean “tamed” especially in the context of the powerful winds, that he was given the ability to control and tame.
Derivative: مُقَرَّنِينَ – مُقَرَّن can mean to be joined and chained. It can also refer to the fastening tight and shackling of chains.
Derivative: الْأَصْفادِ refers to chains and shackles that are used to constrain or restrain someone.

- بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ literally means “Without account”. It is an expression to mean that you are free to dispose of the matter as you see fit by your own discretion and you will not be held to account for how you decide.
Derivative: كَذَّابٌ – كَاذِبٌ is a liar but كَذَّابٌ means someone who is a perpetual liar, someone who lies over and over again.
Derivative: زُلْفَىٰ Closeness and a high status and standing.
Derivative: تَحْنَثْ from حَنِثَ – حِنْث when connected to an oath (اليَمين) means to break it.
Derivative: أَوَّابٌ means to keep turning back to Allah again and again in a continuous and constant manner.
- “Those who were in possession (أُولِي) of great strength (الْأَيْدِي) and foresight, insight and vision (الْأَبْصَار)”.
Derivative: خَالِصَةٍ refers to a special quality.
Derivative: أَخْلَصْنَا – means to purify and cleanse.
Derivative: الدَّارِ referring to الدار الآخرة – The home of the hereafter.
Derivative: الْأَخْيَارِ means “the best” with “مِن” (from) it means “from the very best”. It is the plural of خَيْر which means “better”.

Derivative: عُجَابٌ means the same as عَجِيب (strange or wondrous) and it has been said that it is more far-reaching than عَجِيب i.e., carrying more emphasis in respect to finding wonder and strangeness in a matter.
Derivative: مُّفَتَّحَةً means to be opened wide or held open wide. Referring to the doors or gates of the gardens of bliss implying that those who enter them are welcome and secure.
Derivative: أَتْرَابٌ – The plural of تِرْبٌ which means of similar age and can also imply compatibility and similarity in general i.e., a perfect fit.
Derivative: نَّفَادٍ is the Masdar (مَصْدَر) of نَفِدَ which means to run out, come to an end, expire, become depleted or exhausted.
Derivative: حَمِيمٌ is boiling scalding water.
Derivative: غَسَّاقٌ refers to bodily fluids that have left the body like blood, puss and tears i.e., a foul liquid with the most disgusting taste and smell.
Derivative: أَزْوَاجٌ – refers to kinds and categories i.e., various kinds.
Derivative: فَوْجٌ is a large group of people.
Derivative: مُّقْتَحِمٌ from اِقْتَحَمَ which means to enter with force and speed. Can also mean to plunge into something or invade and breach.

“Indeed, this is a thing or matter which is intended” – This refers to the call of the Messenger ﷺ and يُرَادُ (intended) implies that by his call to Tawheed, he intends and has an agenda for you. This is therefore a statement from the leaders of the Quraish to warn the people away from him by claiming that he ﷺ has a bad plan or intentions for them to turn them away from their idols.
- “The high council of Chiefs” referring to the high council or assembly of Angels.

Derivative: اخْتِلَاقٌ from اِخْتَلَقَ which means to fabricate and make up a lie. It stems from خَلَقَ which means to create. اخْتِلَاقٌ is to create something by making something up without a basis to support it. It means to contrive, invent, concoct or fabricate.
Derivative: ٱلْمِلَّةِ can refer to the Deen (i.e., religion) either of their forefathers or the Christians, or it can refer to the nation that followed a Deen.
Derivative: سَوَّيْتُ – "I proportioned”. It is from سَوَّى which means to level or smooth out, or to balance and make proportionate i.e., to make something into its correct and refined form.

- “All of them (كُلُّهُمْ) All together (أَجْمَعُونَ)” – The use of both of these together emphasises that there were no exceptions i.e., all the Angels without exception prostrated before Adam (as) when Allah commanded them to.

Derivative: إِلَّآ can mean except or apart from however, sometimes it is more correct to understand it as “Not so” or just as a negation for what follows. So, here: إِلَّا إِبْلِيسَ can be understood to mean: “Iblis did not” or “Not so, Iblis”.
Derivative: الْعَالِينَ means those who regard themselves to be higher than others, in arrogance or station.
Derivative: رَجِيمٌ can be understood to mean accursed but literally means the one who is cast out, expelled and pelted (with stones) from رَجَمَ.
Derivative: أَنظِر from أَنْظَرَ which means to give respite and provide some more time i.e., to complete or undertake something.
Derivative: الْمُخْلَصِينَ (Al-Mukhlaseen) are those who have been granted sincerity as opposed to الْمُخْلِصِينَ (Al-Mukhliseen) who are those who are sincere.

Derivative: الْمُتَكَلِّفِينَ is the Ism ul-Fa’il (اِسْمُ الفاعِل) form of تَكَلَّفَ . In origin, it means to attempt something that is beyond one’s capability. From this it has also come to refer to someone who attempts to display or show something that is above his natural ability or someone who makes a claim that is not for him to make i.e., to feign something.
Derivative: نَبَأَ – Means “news”, however, here it refers to the truth of what has been brought and been informed about i.e., in the message.
